skill.name: fda-database
skill.description: Query public openFDA datasets for drugs, devices, recalls, adverse events, regulatory submissions, and substance identifiers.
skill.summary: Access public FDA regulatory data through openFDA queries and Python helper scripts.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high score mainly from markdown code fences, API-key examples, openFDA network calls, cache file writes, and MD5 cache-key generation. Review found no prompt injection, no malicious endpoints, and no real command execution, but the skill should be published with disclosure that it runs Python scripts, calls api.fda.gov, reads FDA_API_KEY, and stores cached responses locally.

medium_findings.title: Intended External API Access With Optional API Key
medium_findings.description: The skill sends queries to the public openFDA API and can include FDA_API_KEY as a request parameter. This is intended functionality, but users should know that query terms and optional API credentials are sent to api.fda.gov.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoint and API-key handling are explicit in executable Python code. The behavior is legitimate for an FDA API client, but it is still a real data-disclosure boundary.

low_findings.title: Local Response Cache Writes Files
low_findings.description: The helper creates a local fda_cache directory and writes API responses as JSON files. This is a normal cache, but users should account for local storage of retrieved public FDA records.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and limited to cache files. No evidence showed arbitrary path writes or persistence outside the configured cache directory.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used Only For Cache Key Generation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography because MD5 hashes cache-key strings. The hash is not used for passwords, signatures, integrity guarantees, or security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable code shows hashlib.md5 used only to derive deterministic cache filenames. I found no evidence of security-sensitive cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby/shell backtick findings map to markdown code fences, inline code, and command examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution in Python code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged locations are documentation code blocks or inline examples. The executable scripts do not contain subprocess, os.system, eval, exec, or shell backtick behavior.

low_findings.title: Promotional Behavioral Instruction
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes guidance to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not malicious, but it is marketplace-relevant promotional behavior that users may want disclosed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit, but it is framed as optional and limited to complex workflows. I found no evidence that it tries to bypass security review or override system instructions.

content.value_statement: FDA regulatory research often requires searching separate public datasets for safety, recalls, labels, and submissions. This skill provides openFDA query guidance and Python helpers for structured regulatory analysis.
actual_capabilities: Query openFDA drug adverse event, labeling, NDC, enforcement, approval, and shortage endpoints.
Search medical device events, 510(k) clearances, PMA records, recalls, classifications, and UDI data.
Retrieve food recall, food adverse event, and animal veterinary adverse event records.
Look up substances by UNII or name, including identifiers and structure fields when available.
Use helper scripts for rate limiting, pagination, response caching, and common FDA query patterns.
Provide reference documentation for endpoint fields, search syntax, and analysis workflows.
limitations: It depends on public openFDA availability, coverage, update schedules, and endpoint-specific field quality.
It does not provide medical advice, regulatory approval decisions, or private FDA data access.
The helper scripts can make network requests and store retrieved responses in a local cache directory.
An FDA API key is optional, but unauthenticated daily request limits are lower.

use_cases.title: Review drug safety signals
use_cases.description: Compare adverse event counts, common reactions, seriousness indicators, labels, and recalls for selected medicines.

use_cases.title: Track device regulatory history
use_cases.description: Search adverse events, recalls, 510(k) clearances, PMA records, classifications, and UDI data for a device.

use_cases.title: Monitor recalls and allergens
use_cases.description: Find enforcement records and adverse event reports related to foods, dietary supplements, and recall reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Find drug events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the FDA database skill to find adverse event reports for [drug name]. Summarize total reports, common reactions, and serious events.

prompt_templates.title: Check product recalls
prompt_templates.prompt: Search openFDA for recalls involving [product or device]. Group results by classification, reason, status, and report date.

prompt_templates.title: Compare safety profiles
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare [drug A], [drug B], and [drug C] using adverse events, serious report rates, labeling warnings, and recent recalls.

prompt_templates.title: Build regulatory dossier
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a regulatory evidence summary for [device or substance]. Include openFDA endpoints searched, key identifiers, approvals, recalls, and data gaps.

output_examples.output: A concise safety summary with adverse event volume, common reaction terms, serious report count, recent recalls, and labeling sections to review.

output_examples.input: Research pacemaker recalls and adverse events.
output_examples.output: A device surveillance brief covering recent event types, recall classifications, affected products, dates, and links between recalls and device identifiers.
A substance profile listing preferred names, UNII identifiers, CAS references when available, molecular details, and related openFDA records.

best_practices: Use precise fielded searches before broad wildcard searches to reduce irrelevant FDA records.
Set FDA_API_KEY only when higher daily limits are needed, and avoid sharing keys in prompts.
Validate important findings against source records because openFDA data can be incomplete or delayed.
anti_patterns: Treating adverse event counts as incidence rates without exposure or reporting bias context.
Using the output as medical advice or a substitute for regulatory review.
Running large broad queries without rate limits, pagination planning, or cache management.

faq.question: Does this skill require an FDA API key?
faq.answer: No. A key is optional, but it increases the daily request limit for openFDA queries.

faq.question: What FDA datasets can it query?
faq.answer: It covers public drug, device, food, animal veterinary, enforcement, submission, and substance datasets available through openFDA.

faq.question: Can it make medical or regulatory decisions?
faq.answer: No. It supports research and summarization, but qualified professionals must interpret medical and regulatory implications.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: The helper scripts send queries to api.fda.gov. They do not show evidence of other external endpoints.

faq.question: Does it store data locally?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python helper can cache openFDA responses in a local fda_cache directory.

faq.question: Can it work in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
